narrative:
A weak front pushed just to our south and west Monday night. It gradually fades off the map today, but leaves us in an unstable air mass. Tuesday is less warm with showers and thundershowers developing (not limited to the afternoon hours). Showers and thundershowers diminish later this afternoon. We remain in this humid, unstable air mass Wednesday with a continuation of occasional showers and thundershowers in play. Coverage of showers and thundershowers is less Thursday, and temperatures will be warmer.

The Monday Message discusses our return to typical June weather, as well as a preview of the movie "Pressure", in theaters now.

2027 Blue Ridge Cameo Photo Winners Announced

In addition to our 15 winning photographs in the Blue Ridge Photo Contest, check out the 36 "Cameo Photo Winners" on the Photo Contest page .

RaysWeather.Com App

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Hi: 71

High 71° Station sponsor Forecast Current Graphics Archives Webcams Showers & Thundershowers Developing Last Updated Tue June 09 at 4:14PM by Ray Russell A weak front pushed just to our south and west Monday night. It gradually fades off the map today, but leaves us in an unstable air mass. Tuesday is less warm with showers and thundershowers developing (not limited to the afternoon hours). Showers and thundershowers diminish later this afternoon. We remain in this humid, unstable air mass Wednesday with a continuation of occasional showers and thundershowers in play. Coverage of showers and thundershowers is less Thursday, and temperatures will be warmer. The Monday Message discusses our return to typical June weather, as well as a preview of the movie "Pressure", in theaters now. 2027 Blue Ridge Cameo Photo Winners Announced In addition to our 15 winning photographs in the Blue Ridge Photo Contest, check out the 36 "Cameo Photo Winners" on the Photo Contest page . Subscribe Monday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Forecast Discussion It's soupy! A backdoor front arrived yesterday and pushed to our south and west Monday night. That front fades out today, leaving us in a humid, unstable air mass. Showers and thundershowers develop today and then diminish late this afternoon. Expect another round of showers and thundershowers Wednesday, especially in the afternoon. Temperatures are less warm today but warmer again Wednesday. Thursday is warmer still, and we'll call thundershower coverage "widely scattered" in the afternoon. A front from the northwest approaches Friday and moves through Friday night. Showers and thundershowers look more prolific to our west Friday, but scattered afternoon coverage is possible here. Temperatures remain toasty. Friday's front stalls just to our east and south. The weekend is drier, but I cannot rule out a shower or thundershower either day. Temperatures are warm, but less warm than Friday. The next front arrives from the west Monday evening. Expect showers and thundershowers Monday, possibly beginning before sunrise. They'll taper off quickly Monday evening. Monday's front brings a more meaningful temperature change. Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters. Subscribe Log in Ray Russell Founder & CEO PHOTO OF THE DAY CLICK TO ENLARGE A fantastic Summer view. Submitted by Susan Murphy. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights Monday Message for June 8, 2026 This week's Monday Message highlights a week of hit-or-miss showers and thundershowers that will be returning to the region in typical June fashion. Also, we discuss the movie "Pressure", in theaters now. Jun 08, 2026 May 2026 Recap Dry, Until It Wasn't The story of Spring 2026 was drought. Many stations experienced their warmest and/or driest April on record, and while the warmth backed off some into May, it took a while for the pattern to finally make a move towards the wetter side of things. But once it did, it did so with a bang. Not everywhere in the area made it to their respective May average precipitation numbers, due to the somewhat hit-or-miss nature of the rainy stretch around Memorial Day, but most recieved 70% or more of a month's worth of rainfall in about a week. No station took advantage of the wetter pattern more than the Asheville Airport, south of downtown. In a night that produced flash flooding damage to roadways around NE Henderson, SE Buncombe, and western Rutherford counties, the airport picked up nearly 3 inches of rain. 2.89", to be exact. That is almost as much as North Wilkesboro picked up for the entire month! As for KAVL's monthly total, it was an impressive 8.62", more than doubling average. It rained so much in the final 12 days of the month that the airport went from being over 8 inches behind normal annual accumulation on May 19th, to being just under 2 inches behind schedule. The graph below shows the numbers for the first 5 months of 2026 in green, as well as the wettest (blue) and driest (red) years, along with average (brown). In the High Country and SW Virginia, things were fairly hit-or-miss, with Boone, Beech Mountain, Wytheville, and Banner Elk all falling between 65 and 85 percent of average monthly rainfall, while Galax's 4.94" exceeded average by 24 percent. As mentioned earlier, the vast majority of this fell in roughly a 7 to 10 day stretch to end the month. In Boone's case, most of it fell in the days centered around the gloomy Memorial Day weekend. You can also see that warm stretch in the mid-month period before we finally made the switch to a wetter pattern, highlighted by four straight highs in the low 80s. Memorial Day Weekend Wedge As we get deeper into the summer, the classic Appalachian "wedge" becomes a more infrequent and less impactful phenomenon. However, late spring and summertime wedges are certainly not unheard of, and we had a doozy just before Memorial Day. Remarkably cool high temperatures invaded the Foothills on the 23rd, when many locations recorded a half inch to an inch of rain. The weather whiplash was strong in Wilkesboro, where three straight days in the 90s was suddenly interrupted by 80s, then 60s, then a high of just 55 degrees on the 23rd! The map below shows the daily rainfall for the 23rd. Record April to Average May If you recall from our April Recap, several locations in our region saw the warmest April on record. In Morganton, the average high temperature for the month of April was right under 80 degrees. What makes that number especially remarkable is that not only is it above average for the months of April and May, but that it actually was slightly higher than the average high temperature for May! In fact, the month-to-month increase in average high temperature from April to May should be about 7 or 8 degrees, depending on where you are. None of the sites listed in the tables below increased by more than 2.1 degrees, and four of them actually decreased (Lenoir, Marshall, Morganton, and North Wilkesboro). By and large, the final numbers for May were about as close to average as you will find. With the remarkable warmth of April being fairly close to May averages, we essentially had "two Mays" in 2026, weather-wise. Local Details The tables and maps below re-iterate the story told above, but at a more local scale. Only a handful of outliers exceed ~1.5F anomalies on average temperature for May. While there was plenty of variance in between, the washed-out average is almost exactly where it usually is.

As mentioned before, precipitation was a bit hit-or-miss with the switch over to summertime convective storms versus the widespread precipitation events of the cold season. However, most managed to get within 20% of normal or exceed it. The "feast or famine" is most obvious with the percentage differences from the Asheville Airport to Mount Airy: 203.7% vs 53.2%.
